questions.question: Explain the structural differences between a singly linked list, a doubly linked list, and a circular linked list. For each type, state one advantage and one disadvantage.
keyPoints: Singly: each node has data and next pointer; O(n) traversal only forward.
Doubly: nodes have next and prev pointers; allows bidirectional traversal but uses extra memory.
Circular: last node's next points to head; eliminates NULL checks but care needed to avoid infinite loops.
Advantage/disadvantage: singly – simple, no extra memory; doubly – easy reverse traversal, double space; circular – no end‑marker, harder termination detection.

questions.question: Write an algorithm (pseudo‑code) to insert a new node at the end of a circular singly linked list that maintains a tail pointer. Include a brief analysis of its time and space complexities.
keyPoints: If list empty: new node points to itself, update tail to new node.
Otherwise: newNode.next = tail.next (head), tail.next = newNode, update tail = newNode.
Time complexity: O(1) – constant number of pointer updates.
Space complexity: O(1) – only a few pointers are used regardless of list size.

questions.question: Provide a complete C function to delete the first occurrence of a node with a given key from a doubly circular linked list. Discuss all possible edge cases (empty list, node is head, node is tail, single node) and give the time‑ and space‑complexity of the operation.
keyPoints: Edge cases: empty list → nothing to delete; single node → after deletion list becomes empty; node is head or tail → update head/tail pointers accordingly; node not found → return indicating failure.
Algorithm steps: locate node (traverse using next until NULL or found), adjust prev.next = node.next, next.prev = node.prev; free node; update head if needed.
Time complexity: O(n) in worst case (search), O(1) if pointer to node is given.
Space complexity: O(1) – only a few temporary pointers are used.

questions.question: Describe Floyd’s Cycle‑Finding Algorithm (tortoise and hare) for detecting a cycle in a singly linked list. Provide pseudo‑code, explain why the algorithm works (proof of correctness), and show how to modify it to return the first node of the cycle (the entrance point). Include a discussion of its time and space complexities.
keyPoints: Algorithm: move slow pointer one step and fast pointer two steps; if they meet, a cycle exists.
Proof: if a cycle exists, fast catches slow after at most μ+λ steps where μ = distance to cycle start, λ = cycle length.
Finding entrance: after detection, reset one pointer to head, move both one step at a time; they meet at cycle start.
Complexities: time O(n), space O(1) – only two pointers required.
